name: org.machanism.machai:machai-mcp-server
description: Machai MCP Server is a Java 17 Maven artifact that provides a server implementation for the Model Context Protocol (MCP) using the Machai AI framework. It integrates Machai GenAI tools with MCP clients and supports both HTTP and STDIO interfaces for AI-powered tool execution, prompt delivery, and orchestration. The project exposes dynamically loaded GenAI functions as MCP tools and prompts and is intended for applications and development environments that need a protocol-compatible bridge between MCP clients and Machai tool providers. The server can run as a local process using standard input and output, or as a remote HTTP service backed by Jetty. HTTP deployments support both stateless request handling and streamable, session-aware communication. The primary package org.machanism.machai.mcp.server contains the command-line entry point, transport-specific server implementations, and the generic adapter that converts Machai tool metadata into MCP tool and prompt specifications. McpServer parses command-line options and starts the appropriate mode: without a port it starts the STDIO server; with a port it starts an HTTP server; and the --session option selects streamable HTTP mode. AbstractMcpServer defines shared server metadata, project directory handling, and the tool specification builder contract used by transport-specific implementations. AbstractHttpMcpServer configures the Jetty server, connector, servlet context, and HTTP transport servlet used by remote MCP deployments. HttpStatelessMcpServer provides a stateless synchronous HTTP MCP server, HttpStreamableMcpServer provides a streamable session-oriented HTTP MCP server, and StdioMcpServer exposes MCP over standard input and output for local integrations and process-based clients. GenericGenaiAdapter registers Machai GenAI tools, builds JSON schemas from parameter descriptors, routes MCP tool calls to Machai ToolFunction instances, converts request arguments to JSON, adds session context when available, executes the underlying function with request arguments, optional session identifiers, the configured project directory, and runtime configuration, then serializes text content results or MCP error results for clients. This artifact does not include its own tools for publication and requires additional libraries implementing functional tools and prompts to supply the capabilities that will be exposed through MCP.

usageContext: Expose dynamically loaded Machai GenAI functions as MCP tools and prompts
Run a local MCP server over standard input and output for process-based clients and development environments
Run a remote MCP server over HTTP using Jetty servlet hosting
Use stateless HTTP request handling for synchronous remote MCP integrations
Use streamable session-aware HTTP communication when clients need session context
Bridge Machai ToolFunction definitions and parameter descriptors into MCP-compatible JSON tool schemas
Route MCP tool invocations to underlying Machai functions with request arguments, optional session identifiers, project directory context, and runtime configuration
Deliver MCP prompts where supported by HTTP server variants
Integrate Java-based AI tool providers with IDE assistants, local agents, automation systems, and backend services
Provide a ready-to-run command-line MCP server through org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.McpServer

constructors.description: Creates a stateless synchronous HTTP MCP server that listens for HTTP requests on a specified port. Use this constructor when exposing Machai GenAI tools to remote MCP clients without streamable session-oriented communication.

examples.description: Creates a stateless HTTP MCP server, configures the current project directory, binds the server to port 8080, registers Machai tools, and starts serving synchronous MCP requests for remote clients.

constructors.description: Creates a streamable, session-aware HTTP MCP server that listens for HTTP requests on a specified port. Use this constructor for HTTP deployments where clients need streamable communication and session context in addition to shared Machai tool registration behavior.

examples.description: Creates a streamable HTTP MCP server, assigns project context, binds to port 8081, registers available GenAI tools, and starts an HTTP endpoint suitable for session-aware MCP clients.

features.name: main
features.description: Command-line entry point for starting the MCP server. It parses options for help, project directory, server name, server version, HTTP port, and session mode. Without a port it starts STDIO mode; with a port it starts HTTP mode; with both port and session mode it uses streamable HTTP behavior.

examples.description: Runs the ready-to-use server in STDIO mode for a local MCP client because no port is supplied. The projectDir option gives loaded tools a project context.
Runs the ready-to-use server over HTTP on port 8080 and enables streamable session-aware HTTP mode. Omit --session to use stateless HTTP mode.

features.name: tools
features.description: Registers available Machai GenAI tools and prompt capabilities with the selected MCP server. During this step, tool parameter metadata is converted into MCP-compatible JSON schema properties so clients can discover and invoke tools correctly.

examples.description: Registers tools before the server is started. This is required when embedding the server so loaded Machai ToolFunction instances become visible as MCP tools.

features.name: start
features.description: Starts the configured MCP server transport. Concrete implementations start either STDIO communication, stateless HTTP handling, or streamable HTTP handling after tools and prompts have been configured.

examples.description: Starts a configured stateless HTTP server on port 8080 after project context and tool registration are complete.

features.name: setProjectDir
features.description: Sets the project directory used by the MCP server and loaded Machai tools. This enables project-aware tool execution, configuration lookup, and filesystem-oriented automation from the selected project root.

examples.description: Configures the server to execute loaded tools against a specific project directory before registering tools and starting the transport.

features.name: setPort
features.description: Configures the TCP port used by HTTP MCP server implementations. This applies to stateless HTTP and streamable HTTP deployments backed by Jetty servlet hosting logic.

examples.description: Sets a custom HTTP port before startup so an MCP client can connect to the server at the expected network endpoint.

customizations.name: AbstractMcpServer
customizations.description: Abstract server base that defines shared server metadata, project-directory handling, tool registration, startup behavior, and the tool specification builder contract used by transport-specific implementations. Extend it to create a new MCP transport while reusing the Machai tool registration lifecycle.

examples.description: Represents the shared extension base for server implementations. Concrete code should subclass it and provide transport-specific behavior.

customizations.name: AbstractHttpMcpServer
customizations.description: HTTP server base that supplies Jetty server, connector, servlet context, and HTTP transport servlet hosting behavior used by remote MCP deployments. Extend it when creating an HTTP transport variant that should reuse the common Jetty infrastructure.

examples.description: Represents the shared HTTP infrastructure base for stateless, streamable, or future HTTP MCP server implementations.

customizations.name: GenericGenaiAdapter
customizations.description: Generic adapter that converts Machai ToolFunction definitions and parameter descriptors into MCP tool schemas and synchronous tool specifications. It registers GenAI tools, builds JSON schemas from parameter descriptors, routes MCP tool calls to Machai ToolFunction instances, and serializes results for MCP clients. It is the primary customization point for bridging additional Machai tool definitions into MCP-visible capabilities.

examples.description: Shows the adapter construction pattern for custom server composition. Production code supplies a real tool specification builder for the selected MCP exchange type.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.AbstractMcpServer
studs.description: Abstract base class for MCP server implementations. It centralizes common metadata, project-directory handling, tool registration, startup behavior, and the transport-specific tool specification builder contract for STDIO and HTTP server modes.

examples.description: Use this abstract class as the superclass for a new MCP server transport that should participate in the same Machai tool registration and lifecycle model.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.AbstractHttpMcpServer
studs.description: Abstract base class for HTTP-based MCP server implementations. It provides shared Jetty server, connector, servlet context, and HTTP transport servlet hosting behavior for stateless and streamable HTTP MCP servers.

examples.description: Use this abstract class as the foundation for an HTTP MCP transport that needs the common Jetty connector and servlet context setup.

examples.title: Install and run the ready-to-use MCP server in STDIO mode
examples.scenario: Use the artifact as a local subprocess launched by an MCP client, IDE assistant, or desktop AI tool.
steps: Install the component by adding the Maven dependency org.machanism.machai:machai-mcp-server:1.2.2-SNAPSHOT to a Java 17 application or obtain the built JAR produced by the package profile.
Ensure the runtime classpath includes machai-mcp-server and its dependencies, including the MCP SDK, genai-client, commons-cli, commons-text, SLF4J, Logback, and Jetty dependencies when needed.
Configure the project directory with --projectDir so loaded tools have the correct project context.
Run org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.McpServer without --port to use STDIO mode.
Configure the MCP client to launch this Java command and communicate through standard input and output.
examples.description: This example shows installation through Maven dependency coordinates and invocation of the ready-to-run server in STDIO mode. It is the default mode when no port is supplied and is appropriate for process-based MCP clients.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run the HTTP MCP server
examples.scenario: Expose Machai tools to remote MCP clients over HTTP using stateless or streamable session-aware transport.
steps: Add the Maven dependency org.machanism.machai:machai-mcp-server:1.2.2-SNAPSHOT or build the executable JAR with the Maven assembly package profile.
Choose a port for the HTTP endpoint, such as 8080.
Set --projectDir to the project whose context should be passed to loaded tools.
Run McpServer with --port for stateless HTTP mode.
Add --session when the MCP client requires streamable session-aware HTTP communication.
examples.snippet:
java -cp machai-mcp-server-1.2.2-SNAPSHOT.jar org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.McpServer --projectDir . --name "Machai MCP Server" --version "1.2.2-SNAPSHOT" --port 8080

java -cp machai-mcp-server-1.2.2-SNAPSHOT.jar org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.McpServer --projectDir . --name "Machai MCP Server" --version "1.2.2-SNAPSHOT" --port 8080 --session
examples.description: The first command starts the HTTP server in stateless mode. The second command enables streamable session-aware HTTP mode. Both commands expose dynamically loaded Machai tools and prompts to MCP clients over a Jetty-backed HTTP server.

examples.title: Embed a stateless HTTP MCP server in Java code
examples.scenario: Use the library API directly inside a Java application that hosts an MCP endpoint.
steps: Add the Maven dependency to the host application.
Create org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.HttpStatelessMcpServer with a server name and version.
Configure project directory and HTTP port.
Call tools() to load and expose Machai GenAI tools.
Call start() to begin serving MCP requests.
examples.description: This how-to-use scenario embeds the server rather than launching the command-line entry point. It is useful for backend services or automation runtimes that manage the MCP server lifecycle programmatically.
